one_line_takeaway: Adv-RM trains an RL attack policy that automatically finds high-reward-but-out-of-distribution 'reward-hacking' responses breaking SOTA reward models (>78-100% success), then reuses them as adversarial training data to make RMs more robust and RLHF 2-3x more stable, at ~3x compute cost.
key_finding: Adv-RM reliably breaks state-of-the-art RMs and reveals distinct, surprising vulnerabilities: Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B awards high scores to responses that merely repeat the prompt; Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70B-Reward is fooled by nonsensical gibberish inserted into long text; and Nemotron-4-340B-Reward gives unexpectedly high scores to responses lacking punctuation. Incorporating adversarial examples into RM training substantially improves robustness and RLHF stability, letting RLHF proceed 2-3x (paper also states ~3x) more steps before reward hacking sets in, with a slight RewardBench improvement.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Adv-RM, an RL-based adversarial training framework for reward models (RMs) used in RLHF alignment. Its own novel contributions are two-fold: (1) an automatic attack that trains an adversarial policy (via RLOO, initialized from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) to generate responses that receive high reward from a target RM yet are out-of-distribution (OOD) and low quality -- the authors claim this is the first method to adversarially attack general RMs without human domain knowledge; and (2) using those discovered adversarial responses to augment RM preference training (SFT response as chosen, adversarial response as rejected), which measurably improves RM robustness and RLHF stability. The authors explicitly distinguish this from prior ideas they cite: Amodei et al. (2016) proposed adversarial reward models conceptually but never implemented them; Kirchner et al. (2024) adversarially train verifiers only for rule-based tasks like GSM8K; and concurrent work Wu et al. (2025, rewordbench) uses manually-defined input transformations rather than a learned attack policy.
threat_model: The security concern is reward hacking during RLHF: because RMs are imperfect proxies with poor OOD generalization, a policy being optimized can discover OOD responses that score highly under the RM but are actually low quality, degrading real alignment. The 'attacker' surface Adv-RM exposes is the RM itself -- Adv-RM is a white-box/gray-box diagnostic attack (it uses the target RM's scores plus a second reference RM for ensemble-disagreement OOD detection) that automatically surfaces these exploitable failure modes, and then a defense that hardens the RM against them. Adversary capability assumed: ability to query the target RM's scalar reward and to train a small policy against it; no access to gold/human labels for the adversarial examples.
limitations: The authors' stated limitations: Adv-RM depends on reward ensembles, so adversarial responses are missed if all RMs jointly (and wrongly) predict high quality, indicating a need for better OOD detection; it incurs ~3x the compute of standard RLHF (672 vs 224 A100 hours for 2 rounds). The ablation on data size shows adversarial data is not very diverse -- adding much more than ~1000 samples hurts downstream performance (overfitting). Attack transferability is weak (some transfer for Skywork-27B, none between the Nemotron RMs). LLM judges may underestimate true attack success because they can be fooled by the same adversarial examples. Downstream gains are mostly in robustness/stability, not best-case reward (only a small best-case improvement).

datasets_used: HelpSteer-2-Preferences (Wang et al. 2024) -- relabeled by the gold RM for the synthetic setup and used directly in the real RLHF setup
RewardBench (used to select RM checkpoints and report final RM scores)
128 held-out prompts for downstream policy judging
quantitative_results: Synthetic attack success (Table 1, strict/train): Adv-RM 92.91% vs RM over-optimization 2.85%, Textfooler 0%, RRM 0%, StyleAdv 0%
Synthetic attack success (Table 1, standard/train): Adv-RM 99.31%, Textfooler 9.57%, RM over-optimization 8.31%, RRM 0%, StyleAdv 0%; Adv-RM test 100% (standard and strict)
Real RMs, human evaluation attack success (Table 2): Adv-RM 100% on Skywork-Gemma-2-27B, 83.01% on Llama-Nemotron-70B, 78.85% on Nemotron-4-340B
Real RMs, DeepSeek-R1 judge (Table 2): Adv-RM 99% (Skywork-27B), 71% (Llama-Nemotron-70B), 76% (Nemotron-340B); Llama-405B judge: 99%, 24%, 82% respectively
Ablation (Table 3, synthetic strict): full Adv-RM 92.91% -> 73.27% without filtering -> 16.41% without unequal RM weights (lambda) -> 0.0% without reward threshold
Uncertainty-quality correlation: Pearson -0.05 over SFT distribution vs -0.70 over SFT+Adv-RM distribution
RewardBench (Table 4): Adv-RM overall 0.8399 vs Baseline 0.8329; Chat-Hard 0.6404 vs 0.6118; Reasoning 0.9070 vs 0.8938; Chat 0.9609 vs 0.9721; Safety 0.8514 vs 0.8541
RLHF can proceed ~3x (real setting: 2-3x) more steps before reward hacking; best downstream performance at ~1000 adversarial samples and 2 rounds of adversarial training
Compute cost: conventional RLHF 224 A100 hours vs Adv-RM (2 rounds) 672 A100 hours (~3x) on A100 80G GPUs
Claimed >80% attack success on SOTA RMs (abstract/intro summary)
baselines_compared: Textfooler (Jin et al. 2020) -- synonym-substitution black-box text attack
RRM (Liu et al. 2024b) -- random cross-prompt response augmentation (used as both attack and robustness baseline)
RM over-optimization (policy trained 3x past best-performing steps)
Ensemble mean-minus-std / UWO (Ens (STD), Coste et al. 2023) -- downstream policy baseline
